questions.prompt: Sequence of events in a story.
questions.explanation: Plot is the structured sequence of events in a narrative. Aristotle's Poetics (335 BCE) defined plot (mythos) as the soul of tragedy, demanding a beginning, middle, and end bound by causality rather than mere chronology. Gustav Freytag's Pyramid (1863) refined this into exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, denouement. On the AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQ, examiners reward students who trace how plot construction generates meaning rather than merely summarizing events; the 6-point rubric awards thesis (1), evidence and commentary (4), and sophistication (1).

Exposition opens a narrative by establishing setting, characters, and inciting situation. Joyce's 'The Dead' (Dubliners, 1914) demonstrates masterful exposition through the Misses Morkans' annual dance, slowly accumulating social and emotional context before Gabriel's epiphany. On the AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQ, students must analyze how authorial choices in exposition shape reader expectations and thematic groundwork; the 6-point rubric rewards a defensible thesis (Row A) plus specific textual evidence tied to interpretive commentary (Row B).

Rising action escalates tension toward the climax through complications and conflict. Freytag's Pyramid (1863) systematizes this for five-act drama; Shakespeare's Macbeth (1606) embodies it as Macbeth murders Duncan, suspects Banquo, and consults the witches. On the AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis and Q2 prose-analysis FRQs, the 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 points for evidence/commentary) rewards students who explain how escalating choices create meaning rather than cataloguing plot beats. Cause-and-effect tracing distinguishes a 3+ essay from a summary.

Climax is the peak of conflict where the protagonist's fate pivots. In Sophocles's Oedipus Rex it is Oedipus's recognition of his patricide and incest; in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) it is Kurtz's 'The horror! The horror!' On the AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQ, examiners want students to identify climax not as a moment of action but as a turning point in characterization or theme. The 6-point rubric's sophistication point (Row C) is earned by demonstrating that the climax's craft choices generate complex interpretive meaning.

Denouement, from French dénouement ('unknotting'), resolves remaining threads after climax. Toni Morrison's Beloved (1987) closes with Sethe's healing in community and the ambiguous 'This is not a story to pass on,' leaving denouement deliberately incomplete to honor traumatic memory. On the AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQ, students argue how an author uses resolution (or its withholding) to shape thematic meaning. The 6-point rubric rewards essays that defend interpretation using selected works rather than recapping endings.

Protagonist is the central character whose journey the narrative traces. Joyce's Gabriel Conroy in 'The Dead' (Dubliners, 1914) is a protagonist whose external composure masks internal paralysis; Achebe's Okonkwo in Things Fall Apart (1958) is a tragic protagonist whose rigidity destroys him as Igbo culture collapses under colonialism. AP Lit Q2 prose FRQs frequently center protagonist analysis: the 6-point rubric awards evidence + commentary (Row B, 4 pts) when students trace how authorial choices in characterization shape the reader's interpretation.

Antagonist embodies the opposing force, person, or system that resists the protagonist. In Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) the Republic of Gilead itself is the antagonist of Offred; in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) Kurtz functions as both antagonist and dark double for Marlow. AP Lit Q2 FRQ analysis (6-point rubric, Row B) rewards students who frame antagonists as instruments of theme — not mere villains. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how the antagonist's role complicates rather than confirms moral certainty.

Round characters are fully developed with contradiction, depth, and capacity for change. E.M. Forster coined the distinction in Aspects of the Novel (1927): flat characters are constructed around a single idea; round characters are 'capable of surprising in a convincing way.' Hurston's Janie Crawford in Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is paradigmatically round, growing through three marriages toward self-possession. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric) reward students who select works with round characters whose complexity supports defensible interpretive claims.

Flat characters are constructed around a single dominant trait. Forster, Aspects of the Novel (1927): they 'never need re-introducing.' Mrs. Bennet in Austen's Pride and Prejudice (1813) is flat — her obsession with marrying daughters never deepens. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs use flat characters as foils: the 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards students who explain how a flat character's simplicity sharpens contrast with a round protagonist's complexity, deepening thematic meaning rather than merely categorizing types.

Dynamic characters change meaningfully across a narrative. Pip in Dickens's Great Expectations (1861), Janie in Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), and Okonkwo in Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958, though his change ends in suicide) are dynamic. Forster (1927) distinguished change from mere event; dynamism implies internal transformation. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who select dynamic characters and trace specific catalysts of change, defending an interpretation that connects character arc to thematic resonance.

Setting is the time and place of a narrative, including historical, cultural, and physical context. Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is inseparable from its rural Florida and Eatonville Black-town setting; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) is inseparable from Gilead's repurposed Cambridge. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who treat setting as an active element shaping character and theme, not mere backdrop. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how setting carries cultural or historical critique.

Mood is the atmospheric feeling a setting evokes in a reader. Poe's 'The Fall of the House of Usher' (1839) sustains a mood of decay through 'mere house, and the simple landscape features of the domain.' García Márquez's Macondo in One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) carries a mood of magical melancholy. AP Lit Q1 poetry FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward identifying how diction, imagery, and syntax construct mood; sophistication (Row C) emerges when mood is shown to complicate rather than confirm thematic readings.

Cultural context is the social, historical, and ideological backdrop shaping characters' choices. Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) cannot be read without precolonial Igbo religion, kinship, and law; Morrison's Beloved (1987) cannot be read without the lived afterlife of slavery. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric) reward students who ground interpretation in cultural context, defending claims with specific textual evidence (Row B, 4 pts) tied to historical reality without lapsing into background-only summary.

Pathetic fallacy is John Ruskin's term (Modern Painters III, 1856) for landscape that mirrors human emotion — the storm raging when Lear rages on the heath in Shakespeare's King Lear (1606). Heaney's 'Storm on the Island' deploys it; Brontë's Wuthering Heights (1847) saturates moor and weather with character feeling. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who name pathetic fallacy precisely and explain how the device fuses inner and outer worlds, generating thematic depth.

Symbolic setting is a place whose physical features carry meaning beyond the literal. Conrad's Congo River in Heart of Darkness (1899) is geography and psychology at once — an inward journey via outward water. Morrison's 124 Bluestone Road in Beloved (1987) is a haunted house and a haunted history. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who treat setting as participatory in theme. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when symbolic setting reveals interpretive complexity rather than confirming the obvious.

Narrator is the voice telling a story, distinct from the author. The narrator may be first-person (a character) or third-person (external). Marlow in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) is a framed first-person narrator nested inside an unnamed outer narrator. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who distinguish narrator from author and explain how narrative voice mediates meaning. Sophistication (Row C) emerges when students show how the narrator's perspective shapes — and limits — the reader's understanding.

First-person narration uses 'I' (or 'we') and limits readers to one character's perspective. Offred narrates Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) in fragmented first-person, mirroring her constrained Gilead reality. Huck narrates Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) in vernacular. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who interrogate first-person reliability and explain how voice constructs theme; the sophistication point (Row C) is earned when limitation becomes interpretive lever.

Third-person narration uses 'he,' 'she,' 'they' and stands outside characters. It may be limited (one consciousness) or omniscient (multiple). Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) uses third-person free indirect discourse, drifting in and out of Janie's mind. Morrison's Beloved (1987) shifts among Sethe, Denver, Paul D, and Beloved herself. AP Lit Q2 FRQs (6-point rubric) reward students who name the specific third-person mode and trace how narrative distance generates meaning.

Omniscient narration grants the storyteller access to all characters' thoughts and events across time. Tolstoy's Anna Karenina (1877), García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967), and George Eliot's Middlemarch (1872) deploy classic omniscient narration. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain how omniscient access — the choice of which minds to enter and when — constructs sympathy, irony, and theme. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show that 'all-knowing' is itself a constructed authorial choice.

Unreliable narrator is Wayne C. Booth's term (The Rhetoric of Fiction, 1961) for a narrator whose account the reader must distrust. Nabokov's Humbert Humbert in Lolita (1955) and the governess in James's The Turn of the Screw (1898) are paradigmatic. Atwood's Offred admits her own revisions. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who identify specific textual cues of unreliability — contradiction, omission, self-justification — and earn sophistication (Row C) by showing how unreliability complicates rather than blocks meaning.

Theme is the underlying meaning or insight a work conveys, distinct from subject. Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) has the subject of colonialism but the theme of how rigid masculinity and cultural inflexibility compound catastrophe. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric) require students to argue interpretive claims about theme, not merely identify them. Row A (1 pt) rewards a defensible thesis; Row B (4 pts) evidence-and-commentary; Row C (1 pt) sophistication — earned when thematic claim demonstrates interpretive complexity.

Universal theme is an insight applicable beyond a specific work's setting — the costs of pride, the fragility of innocence, the persistence of memory. Shakespeare's Hamlet (1601) makes universal claims about indecision and mortality; Morrison's Beloved (1987) about memory's burden. AP Lit Q3 FRQs (6-point rubric) reward students who argue universal themes through specific works rather than abstract generalization. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when the universal is grounded in concrete textual evidence, not floated as platitude.

Coming-of-age (Bildungsroman) traces a young protagonist's moral, psychological, and social maturation. The form began with Goethe's Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (1795–96); modern exemplars include Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916) and Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), in which Janie matures across three marriages. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric) frequently include coming-of-age prompts. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned by interrogating what the protagonist 'comes of age' into — and at what cost.

Moral struggle is the inner conflict over right action that drives much canonical literature. Hamlet's hesitation to revenge his father (Shakespeare, 1601), Huck's choice to 'go to hell' rather than betray Jim (Twain, 1885), and the speaker of Frost's 'The Road Not Taken' (1916) all dramatize moral struggle. AP Lit Q3 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace specific choices and consequences; sophistication (Row C) emerges when moral struggle illuminates broader thematic complexity.

questions.prompt: Theme of fate vs free will.
questions.explanation: Determinism is the philosophical position that events — including human choices — are causally determined by prior conditions. In literature it surfaces as fate (Sophocles's Oedipus), heredity and environment (Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles, 1891), or social structure (Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale, 1985, where Gilead determines Offred's options). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue the work's stance on determinism vs free will using specific evidence, earning sophistication (Row C) through complex synthesis.

Sonnet is a 14-line lyric in iambic pentameter, originated by Giacomo da Lentini in 13th-century Sicily, perfected by Petrarch (Canzoniere, 14th c.), and naturalized in English by Wyatt, Surrey, and Shakespeare (1609 Sonnets). The Petrarchan octave-sestet structure and Shakespearean three-quatrains-plus-couplet structure are both fair game. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric) regularly anchor on sonnets. Row B (4 pts) rewards students who explain how the form's compression and turn generate meaning, not merely identify rhyme scheme.

Tercet is a three-line stanza, often rhyming aaa, aba, or unrhymed. Dante's Divine Comedy (c. 1308–21) uses terza rima (aba bcb cdc); Frost's 'Acquainted with the Night' (1928) and 'Provide, Provide' use tercets; Heaney's translations preserve them. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain how stanza form generates meaning — the tercet's incompleteness compared with a quatrain often dramatizes restlessness or interlocking thought. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when stanza shape becomes interpretive lever.

Quatrain is a four-line stanza, the most common English stanza form. Ballad meter (abcb, alternating tetrameter/trimeter) is a quatrain form Dickinson used relentlessly; the Shakespearean sonnet stacks three quatrains plus a couplet. Yeats's 'The Second Coming' (1920) uses loose quatrain-like blocks. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric) reward students who explain how quatrain structure — its rhyme scheme, line lengths, and closure or open-endedness — generates meaning. Sophistication (Row C) emerges when form is interpretive evidence, not decoration.

Couplet is two consecutive rhyming lines forming a complete thought. Heroic couplets — pairs of rhyming iambic pentameter lines — dominated 18th-century English verse via Pope (An Essay on Man, 1733–34) and Dryden. The Shakespearean sonnet's concluding couplet serves as epigrammatic punctuation. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain how the couplet's brevity and closure generate aphoristic force or ironic reversal — particularly the volta-couplet of Shakespearean sonnets, where a 12-line argument can be undermined or amplified in two lines.

Free verse is poetry without regular meter or rhyme scheme. Walt Whitman's Leaves of Grass (1855) opened the modern American tradition; T.S. Eliot's 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915) and The Waste Land (1922) refined it; H.D. and Pound's Imagist work refused fixed meter. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain that free verse is not formless: it has rhythm, line breaks, and syntactic patterning that generate meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how line breaks function as interpretive cues.

Meter is the recurring pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables organizing a line of verse. English meter is accentual-syllabic (counting both stress and syllable count); Greek and Latin meters counted vowel length. Scansion marks each syllable as stressed (/) or unstressed (u) and groups them into feet. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who scan accurately and explain how meter generates meaning — variations, substitutions, and pauses are interpretive evidence, not technical trivia.

Iamb is a metrical foot of one unstressed syllable followed by one stressed syllable (u /), as in 'to-DAY' or 'be-LIEVE.' It is the dominant foot of English verse because English speech rhythm naturally falls into iambic patterns. Shakespeare's blank verse, Donne's Holy Sonnets, and Frost's 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening' (1923) all use iambs. AP Lit Q1 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who identify iambic patterns and explain how variations (trochaic inversion, spondee) generate emphasis and meaning.

Trochee is a metrical foot of one stressed syllable followed by one unstressed syllable (/ u), as in 'TI-ger' or 'GAR-den.' Trochaic meter has a falling, often urgent or driving rhythm. Blake's 'The Tyger' (1794) uses trochaic tetrameter ('Tyger, Tyger, burning bright'); Longfellow's Song of Hiawatha (1855) uses trochaic tetrameter throughout. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who recognize trochaic substitution in predominantly iambic verse as a meaning-bearing rhythmic disruption.

Iambic pentameter is a line of five iambic feet (u / u / u / u / u /), the foundational meter of English Renaissance verse. Shakespeare's plays and sonnets, Milton's Paradise Lost (1667, blank verse), and Wordsworth's Prelude (1850) use it. The line is roughly the length of an English breath. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify the meter precisely and explain how variations — feminine endings, trochaic inversions, caesurae — generate dramatic emphasis or interpretive shifts.

questions.prompt: Foot with two unstressed then stressed.
questions.explanation: Anapest is a metrical foot of two unstressed syllables followed by one stressed (u u /), as in 'un-der-STAND.' Anapestic meter has a galloping, propulsive rhythm. Byron's 'The Destruction of Sennacherib' (1815) uses anapestic tetrameter ('The Assyrian came down like the wolf on the fold'); much of Lewis Carroll's verse uses it. AP Lit Q1 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who identify anapestic substitution and explain how the three-syllable rush generates speed, frivolity, or, in elegies, breathless lament.

Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in nearby words. Old English verse (Beowulf, c. 8th–11th c.) was built on alliterative meter; Gerard Manley Hopkins revived its density in 19th-century English ('Glory be to God for dappled things,' 'Pied Beauty,' 1877). Seamus Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) uses alliteration to bind sound to action ('squelch and slap'). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how alliteration generates emphasis, mood, or thematic linkage — not merely list the device.

Assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds in nearby words, regardless of consonants. Dickinson uses assonance throughout ('A narrow Fellow in the Grass') to create internal music without rhyme. Yeats's 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree' (1890) deploys long-i and long-o assonance to slow the line. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who name assonance precisely and explain how vowel music generates mood, slowness, or harmonic resonance. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when assonance becomes interpretive evidence.

Consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds at the end (or interior) of words, often without matching vowels. Hopkins's 'pitched past pitch of grief' (sonnet '(Carrion Comfort),' 1885) uses consonance to bind tonal weight; Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) repeats hard 'k' and 't' sounds. AP Lit Q1 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish consonance from alliteration (positional) and from assonance (vowels) and explain how the device generates rhythmic and thematic effect. Sophistication (Row C) is earned by showing interpretive payoff.

Onomatopoeia is the use of words that imitate sounds — 'buzz,' 'clang,' 'murmur,' 'splash.' Tennyson's 'The murmuring of innumerable bees' ('Come Down, O Maid,' 1847) is a textbook line. Hopkins's 'sprung rhythm' often deploys onomatopoeia to fuse sound and meaning. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who identify onomatopoeia and explain how sonic imitation reinforces thematic content. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when onomatopoeia is shown to do more than decorate — when sound carries argument.

questions.prompt: Pattern of stresses in a line.
questions.explanation: Rhythm is the felt pattern of stresses across a line, broader than meter — including caesura, enjambment, and substitutions. Hopkins coined 'sprung rhythm' (preface to Poems, posthumous 1918) to describe stress-counted but syllable-flexible patterns mimicking speech. Free verse poets like Whitman and Eliot construct rhythm without regular meter. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who attend to rhythm holistically — not merely scansion — and explain how disruptions generate emphasis and meaning.

Metaphor is a comparison without 'like' or 'as,' asserting that one thing IS another. I.A. Richards (The Philosophy of Rhetoric, 1936) named the parts: tenor (subject) and vehicle (image). Eliot's 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915) compares evening to 'a patient etherized upon a table' — a startling vehicle that announces modernist disorientation. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who unpack tenor and vehicle and explain how metaphor's compression generates thematic complexity. Sophistication (Row C) emerges when interpretation traces metaphor's resonance.

Simile is a comparison using 'like' or 'as.' Frost's 'My Heart Leaps Up' simile imagery, Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) 'I have always been scared of you, / With your Luftwaffe, your gobbledygoo,' and Eliot's 'Let us go then, you and I, / When the evening is spread out against the sky / Like a patient etherized upon a table' (1915) all anchor on simile. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who distinguish simile from metaphor and explain why explicit comparison serves a particular interpretive purpose.

Conceit is an extended, elaborate metaphor sustained through a poem, characteristic of the metaphysical poets — Donne, Herbert, Marvell. Donne's 'A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning' (1633) develops the conceit of two lovers as the legs of a drafting compass; Herbert's 'The Pulley' personifies God's gifts. T.S. Eliot's essay 'The Metaphysical Poets' (1921) revived their reputation. AP Lit Q1 poetry FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the conceit and trace its sustained logic across stanzas; sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned by interpretive payoff.

Personification gives human traits to nonhuman entities — natural forces, abstract concepts, objects. Donne's 'Death, be not proud' (Holy Sonnet 10, 1633) personifies death to defeat it; Dickinson's 'Because I could not stop for Death' (#479) personifies Death as a courteous suitor. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who name personification and explain how the device reorders the relation between human and nonhuman — generating intimacy, irony, or thematic argument. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through interpretive nuance.

Apostrophe is direct address to an absent person, abstract concept, or nonhuman entity. Shelley's 'Ode to the West Wind' (1820) ('O wild West Wind, thou breath of Autumn's being'), Donne's Holy Sonnets to Death, and Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) ('You do not do, you do not do / Any more, black shoe') all deploy apostrophe. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how apostrophe constructs the speaker's stance — defiance, mourning, invocation — and generates thematic argument through direct address.

Speaker is the persona voicing a poem, distinct from the poet. Browning's dramatic monologues — 'My Last Duchess' (1842), 'Porphyria's Lover' — pioneered the technique of constructing a speaker whose self-revelation exceeds his self-knowledge. T.S. Eliot's J. Alfred Prufrock (1915) speaks neuroses he barely owns. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish speaker from poet and analyze how the speaker's voice — diction, tone, syntax — generates meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when speaker analysis becomes interpretive lever.

Tone is the speaker's attitude toward subject, audience, or self, conveyed through diction, syntax, and imagery. Hopkins's 'Spring and Fall' (1880) shifts from tender to elegiac to philosophical in 15 lines. Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) carries a tone simultaneously enraged and incantatory. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who identify tone precisely (specific adjectives: melancholic, defiant, sardonic) and explain how authorial choices construct it. Sophistication (Row C) emerges when tone is shown to shift, generating thematic complication.

Mood in poetry is the emotional atmosphere a poem evokes in the reader, distinct from but shaped by tone. Yeats's 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree' (1890) creates a mood of pastoral longing; Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) creates a mood of fragmented anxiety. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish mood (in the reader) from tone (the speaker's attitude) and explain how diction, imagery, and rhythm construct mood. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when mood is tied to thematic argument.

Diction is word choice — formal vs colloquial, abstract vs concrete, Latinate vs Anglo-Saxon. Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) deliberately mixes Anglo-Saxon physicality ('squelch,' 'slap') with abstract Latinate reflection. Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) uses nursery-rhyme diction to carry adult fury. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who name specific diction patterns and explain their thematic effect. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when diction becomes interpretive evidence — not merely 'fancy words.'

Syntax in poetry is the arrangement of words and clauses, including sentence length, fragmentation, inversion, and enjambment. Hopkins's compressed, syntactically distorted lines ('Glory be to God for dappled things,' 'Pied Beauty,' 1877) fuse form and theme. Emily Dickinson's dashes and fragmentary syntax generate semantic openness. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who attend to syntax as a meaning-bearing device — explaining how unusual ordering, inversion, or sentence rhythm generates thematic effect.

Five-act structure organizes a play into exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and denouement. Gustav Freytag's Pyramid (Die Technik des Dramas, 1863) formalized it; Shakespeare's mature plays (Hamlet 1601, Macbeth 1606, King Lear 1606, Othello 1604) follow it loosely. Classical Greek tragedy used different conventions, but Aristotle's Poetics (335 BCE) already prefigured beginning-middle-end logic. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs and Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain how five-act architecture generates dramatic meaning, not merely identify it.

Exposition in drama supplies background — setting, character relationships, prior events — typically in the opening scene. Shakespeare's Hamlet (1601) opens on Elsinore's battlements with the ghost's appearance and Horatio's catechism, supplying political context (Fortinbras), familial tension, and supernatural stakes. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze how exposition's choices shape interpretive trajectory. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how exposition embeds the play's central thematic argument from the start.

Rising action in drama escalates conflict through choices and complications. Macbeth's murder of Duncan, his suspicion of Banquo, and his return to the witches all constitute rising action in Macbeth (1606). Freytag (1863) placed it between exposition and climax. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who trace how specific choices in rising action generate tension and theme — not summarize plot. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how rising action prefigures the climax's interpretive payoff.

Climax in drama is the turning point at which the protagonist's fate is decided. Aristotle's Poetics (335 BCE) located it at peripeteia (reversal) combined with anagnorisis (recognition). In Oedipus Rex (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE) the climax is Oedipus's discovery of his identity; in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) it is the smothering of Desdemona. AP Lit Q2 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify climax as a transformation of meaning, not action alone. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through interpretive complexity.

Denouement (French: 'unknotting') resolves remaining plot threads after climax. Shakespearean tragedies conclude with corpses and political restoration — Fortinbras enters at the end of Hamlet (1601), Malcolm at the end of Macbeth (1606). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue how denouement choices shape thematic meaning — what is unresolved, what is restored, what the audience is left to interpret. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through complex interpretive synthesis.

Aristotle (384–322 BCE) defined tragedy in the Poetics (335 BCE) as 'an imitation of an action that is serious and complete, of a certain magnitude... effecting through pity and fear the catharsis of such emotions.' He identified six elements: plot, character, thought, diction, music, spectacle. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis and Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who anchor tragic analysis in Aristotelian terms — hamartia, peripeteia, anagnorisis, catharsis — and earn sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) by interrogating, not just applying, the framework.

Hamartia is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the tragic protagonist's error or flaw that precipitates catastrophe — often translated 'tragic flaw' but better rendered 'missing the mark.' Oedipus's hubris and Macbeth's ambition are classic hamartiai. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name hamartia precisely and trace its operation through specific scenes. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students interrogate whether hamartia is moral failure or structural inevitability.

Catharsis is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the audience's purgation of pity and fear through witnessing tragedy. The exact mechanism — medical purge, emotional release, intellectual clarification — has been debated since the Renaissance. Hamlet's, Othello's, and Lear's deaths in Shakespeare's tragedies aim at catharsis. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who deploy catharsis as interpretive frame. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when students question what catharsis achieves and at what cost.

Anagnorisis is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the protagonist's moment of recognition — the discovery of a previously hidden truth that transforms understanding. Oedipus's recognition of his patricide and incest in Oedipus Rex (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE) is the paradigmatic instance; Lear's recognition of Cordelia's love (Shakespeare, 1606) is another. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate anagnorisis precisely and explain how recognition reshapes character, plot, and theme simultaneously.

Peripeteia is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the sudden reversal of fortune at the climax of tragedy. It works most powerfully when fused with anagnorisis — as in Oedipus Rex (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE), where Oedipus's discovery and his fall happen in the same moment. Macbeth's peripeteia comes when Birnam Wood moves to Dunsinane and he learns Macduff was 'from his mother's womb / Untimely ripped' (1606). AP Lit Q2 FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who name peripeteia and explain how reversal generates tragic meaning.

Comedy is a dramatic genre ending in happy resolution — usually marriage, reunion, or restored social order — defined in opposition to tragedy. Aristotle's Poetics (335 BCE) treated comedy briefly; the lost second book was the focus of Umberto Eco's The Name of the Rose. Shakespearean comedies (A Midsummer Night's Dream c. 1595, Much Ado About Nothing 1598, Twelfth Night 1601) follow the pattern. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat comedic resolution as interpretive evidence, not merely formal closure.

Comedy of manners satirizes the conventions and affectations of a particular social class — typically the aristocracy or upper-middle class. Sheridan's The School for Scandal (1777), Wilde's The Importance of Being Earnest (1895), and Restoration comedies by Congreve (The Way of the World, 1700) are exemplary. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how comedy of manners critiques social hierarchy through verbal wit and structural irony. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when satire is shown to do interpretive work.

Farce is comedy emphasizing absurd situations, mistaken identity, slapstick, and improbable coincidence. Plautus's Roman comedies and Molière's The Imaginary Invalid (1673) anchor the tradition; Wilde's The Importance of Being Earnest (1895) elevates farce with epigrammatic wit. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how farce's absurdity generates thematic critique — exposing the artificiality of social conventions, the fragility of identity, or the arbitrariness of authority.

Comedy of humours is Ben Jonson's term (Every Man in His Humour, 1598; Every Man Out of His Humour, 1599) for comedy in which characters are dominated by a single 'humour' — choler, melancholy, phlegm, or blood — that produces predictable comic behavior. The form draws on Galenic medicine. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who explain how the humours framework constructs flat characters whose contrasts illuminate thematic argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges by tying the form to its historical moment.

questions.prompt: Dark comedy blending tragic and comic.
questions.explanation: Tragicomedy blends tragic and comic elements, typically with serious threats resolved nontragically. Shakespeare's late 'problem plays' — Measure for Measure (1604), All's Well That Ends Well (c. 1604), and the romances Pericles, The Winter's Tale (1611), Cymbeline, and The Tempest (1611) — bend tragic momentum toward reconciliation. Beckett's Waiting for Godot (1953) modernized the form. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue how tragicomedy's hybrid generates interpretive complexity.

Soliloquy is a dramatic convention in which a character, alone on stage, speaks thoughts aloud to the audience. Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' (Hamlet 3.1, 1601) is the most famous instance; Macbeth's 'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow' (Macbeth 5.5, 1606) ranks alongside. The convention grants the audience interior access. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze soliloquy as carefully crafted self-revelation — not merely 'inner thought' — and trace how its rhetoric constructs character and theme.

Aside is a brief comment a character makes directly to the audience (or to one other character) that the rest of the stage characters do not hear. Iago's asides in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) — 'O, you are well tuned now! But I'll set down the pegs that make this music' — make him the audience's confidant. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how asides construct dramatic irony, granting the audience knowledge the onstage characters lack.

Monologue is an extended speech by a character to other characters (or to the audience as a presumed listener). Distinct from soliloquy (alone on stage), monologue is delivered within the dramatic scene. Hamlet's instructions to the players (Hamlet 3.2, 1601) and Marc Antony's funeral oration in Julius Caesar (Shakespeare, 1599) are exemplary. AP Lit Q2 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who distinguish monologue from soliloquy and explain how the chosen audience — onstage characters vs the theater audience — shapes rhetorical strategy and thematic meaning.

Stichomythia (Greek for 'line-by-line speech') is rapid alternating dialogue in single lines, often heightening conflict. Sophocles uses it; Shakespeare's Richard III (c. 1593) deploys it in the wooing of Lady Anne; Hamlet's quick exchanges with Claudius after Polonius's death (Hamlet 4.3, 1601) work similarly. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the device precisely and explain how clipped dialogue generates tension, power struggle, or interrogation rhythm.

Chorus in Greek tragedy is a group of performers — typically twelve or fifteen in Sophocles — who comment on action, voice communal response, and bridge the human and divine. The chorus in Sophocles's Antigone (c. 441 BCE) and Euripides's The Bacchae (c. 405 BCE) shapes audience interpretation. Modern adaptations: T.S. Eliot's Murder in the Cathedral (1935). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how chorus mediates between play and audience, constructing thematic argument.

Stage directions are the playwright's instructions describing setting, character action, and tone, conventionally italicized in published scripts. Shakespeare's stage directions are sparse ('Exit, pursued by a bear,' The Winter's Tale 3.3, 1611); Ibsen's and Shaw's are elaborate, embedding interpretive commentary. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat stage directions as authored text generating meaning — not metadata. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when stage directions are shown to carry thematic argument or tonal cues.

Breaking the fourth wall is the convention in which a character or actor directly acknowledges the audience, rupturing dramatic illusion. The fourth wall is a metaphor coined by Diderot (Discourse on Dramatic Poetry, 1758). Shakespeare's epilogues (As You Like It c. 1599, The Tempest 1611) break the wall; Brecht's epic theater theorized the rupture as Verfremdungseffekt. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how breaking the fourth wall generates thematic critique by exposing theatrical artifice itself.

Props are physical objects on stage that carry plot, character, or thematic weight. Yorick's skull in Hamlet (5.1, 1601) condenses memento mori philosophy into a stage object; the handkerchief in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) literalizes the play's epistemology of evidence and trust. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat props as symbolic objects whose recurrence and exchange generate meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when prop analysis becomes interpretive lever.

Dramatic repetition is the deliberate restaging or recall of an earlier event, action, or line for cumulative meaning. Lear's repeated 'Never, never, never, never, never' (King Lear 5.3, 1606) over Cordelia's body fuses grief with metrical pentameter rhythm. Beckett's Waiting for Godot (1953) is built on cyclic repetition. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B) reward students who identify repetition as deliberate craft and explain how recursion generates thematic emphasis, irony, or psychological depth.

Dramatic irony is the gap between what characters know and what the audience knows, generated through prior information the audience holds. Oedipus's curse on Laius's killer (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE) hits the audience harder than him; Iago's flattery of Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) is excruciating to watch. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish dramatic irony from verbal and situational irony and explain how the device constructs tragic momentum and audience complicity.

Conflict is the struggle between opposing forces that drives narrative. Aristotle's Poetics (335 BCE) located it at the heart of plot. Conflict may be external (between characters, between character and environment, between character and society) or internal (within a character's psyche). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name specific conflicts and explain how their construction generates theme. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when conflict is shown to layer — external pressures expose internal divisions — generating interpretive complexity.

Person-versus-person conflict pits one character against another. Iago against Othello (Shakespeare, 1604), Atticus Finch against Bob Ewell (Lee, To Kill a Mockingbird, 1960), Sethe against schoolteacher (Morrison, Beloved, 1987). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how person-versus-person conflict carries broader thematic argument — the conflict between Iago and Othello is also a conflict between epistemologies, between truth-telling and storytelling. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Internal conflict is the struggle within a character's mind between competing impulses, values, or identities. Hamlet's hesitation over revenge (Shakespeare, 1601) is internal conflict between action and contemplation; Huck Finn's decision to 'go to hell' rather than betray Jim (Twain, 1885) dramatizes internal moral conflict. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace internal conflict through specific textual evidence — soliloquy, free indirect discourse, juxtaposition — and explain how irresolution generates thematic depth.

Person-versus-nature conflict pits character against environment. Crane's 'The Open Boat' (1898), Hemingway's The Old Man and the Sea (1952), and McCarthy's The Road (2006) anchor the tradition; Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) extends nature into psychological terrain. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how natural antagonism dramatizes broader thematic questions — about indifference, mortality, civilization's fragility. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when nature is shown to function symbolically.

Person-versus-society conflict pits character against institutions, conventions, or collective norms. Hester Prynne against Puritan Boston (Hawthorne, The Scarlet Letter, 1850), Offred against Gilead (Atwood, The Handmaid's Tale, 1985), Janie Crawford against communal expectation (Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God, 1937). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue how individual-society conflict carries social and political critique. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when conflict is shown to expose ideology.

Symbol is a concrete object or image representing an abstract idea. Hawthorne's scarlet letter A (The Scarlet Letter, 1850), Conrad's Congo River (Heart of Darkness, 1899), and Morrison's chokecherry tree on Sethe's back (Beloved, 1987) carry symbolic weight beyond their literal presence. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name specific symbols and trace their interpretive resonance across a work. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students show how a symbol's meaning shifts, generating thematic complexity.

Motif is a recurring symbolic element — image, phrase, situation — that gathers thematic weight through repetition. Hamlet's references to disease and decay (Shakespeare, 1601), the color white in Melville's Moby-Dick (1851), and the rememory motif in Morrison's Beloved (1987) are exemplary. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish motif from symbol (motif emphasizes recurrence) and trace how repetition generates cumulative meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive layering.

Allegory is an extended narrative in which characters, events, and settings systematically represent abstract concepts. Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress (1678) allegorizes Christian salvation; Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) allegorizes the Russian Revolution; Spenser's The Faerie Queene (1590–96) is encyclopedic political-religious allegory. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who distinguish allegory from symbolism (allegory is systematic) and explain how allegorical mapping constructs thematic argument.

Archetype is a universal symbol or character type that recurs across cultures — hero, mentor, shadow, threshold guardian. Carl Jung (The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious, 1959) theorized archetypes as patterns of the collective unconscious; Northrop Frye (Anatomy of Criticism, 1957) systematized literary archetypes. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name specific archetypes and explain how an author deploys, complicates, or subverts archetypal patterns to generate thematic argument.

Religious symbol draws on Jewish, Christian, or other faith traditions to layer meaning — the cross, the fall, the deluge, the wilderness journey. Hawthorne's The Scarlet Letter (1850) saturates Puritan iconography; Morrison's Beloved (1987) weaves Christian baptism and African ancestral memory; Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) interlaces Christian, Buddhist, and Grail symbols. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who unpack specific religious symbols and explain how their resonance shapes thematic claim.

Foreshadowing is the placement of hints suggesting later events. Shakespeare's witches' prophecies in Macbeth (1606) foreshadow the play's trajectory; the opening line of García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) ('Many years later, as he faced the firing squad...') foreshadows by simultaneous flash-forward. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name foreshadowing precisely and explain how its construction generates dramatic irony, suspense, or thematic argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive nuance.

Flashback is a narrative shift to an earlier event, interrupting chronological order to supply context or contrast. Faulkner's The Sound and the Fury (1929) uses flashback as structural principle; Morrison's Beloved (1987) folds slavery's past into Sethe's present through 'rememory.' AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how flashback structure — its placement, frequency, and narrative voice — generates thematic meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when temporal disorder is shown to mirror thematic concern.

Atmospheric foreshadowing uses setting, weather, or mood to anticipate later events. The storm preceding Duncan's murder in Macbeth (Shakespeare, 1606), the gathering clouds in Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles (1891), and the heavy summer heat in 'A Rose for Emily' (Faulkner, 1930) all foreshadow through atmosphere. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the technique precisely and explain how environmental cues prepare reader expectation without explicit telling. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through layered analysis.

Symbolic foreshadowing uses recurring objects or images to anticipate outcomes. The handkerchief in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) foreshadows tragic miscommunication; the ghost in Hamlet (1601) foreshadows the bloodletting to come. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how symbols introduced early acquire foreshadowing weight as the narrative progresses. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when students trace how a symbol's accumulating meanings shape interpretive trajectory.

Dialogic foreshadowing embeds anticipation in characters' speech — a casual remark, a prophecy, a warning. The witches' 'Fair is foul, and foul is fair' (Macbeth 1.1, 1606), Iago's 'I am not what I am' (Othello 1.1, 1604), and Casca's account of portents in Julius Caesar (Shakespeare, 1599) all foreshadow through dialogue. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify dialogic foreshadowing precisely and explain how character speech constructs dramatic irony for the attentive reader.

Verbal irony is the rhetorical figure in which what is said differs from what is meant. Mark Antony's repeated 'Brutus is an honourable man' in Julius Caesar (Shakespeare, 1599) is verbal irony marshalled to political force; Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) sustains verbal irony at book length. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis and Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name verbal irony precisely and explain how the gap between literal statement and intended meaning generates thematic critique.

Situational irony is the rhetorical figure in which outcomes contradict expectations. Oedipus's flight from Corinth to avoid the prophecy delivers him into it (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE); the gift exchange in O. Henry's 'The Gift of the Magi' (1905) renders both gifts useless. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish situational irony from verbal and dramatic irony and explain how reversal of expectation generates thematic argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Dramatic irony is the gap between what characters know and what the audience (or reader) knows, generated by prior narrative information. Sophocles's Oedipus Rex (c. 429 BCE) saturates the play with dramatic irony; Iago's manipulation of Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) is excruciating because the audience sees what Othello cannot. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how dramatic irony constructs reader complicity and how its release at climax generates tragic catharsis.

Satire is a literary mode using mockery, exaggeration, or sustained irony to critique vice, folly, or institutional failure. Swift's Gulliver's Travels (1726) and 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) anchor the English tradition; Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) satirizes antebellum racism; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) and Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949) satirize totalitarianism. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how satiric distance generates thematic critique. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through interpretive nuance.

Sarcasm is a sharp, often bitter form of verbal irony intended to mock or wound. Distinguished from satire (which targets systems) by its personal address. Many of Hamlet's exchanges with Polonius (Shakespeare, 1601) are sarcastic; Beatrice and Benedick exchange sarcasm in Much Ado About Nothing (1598). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish sarcasm from satire and verbal irony and explain how a sarcastic register generates characterization, social critique, or emotional defense.

Short story is a prose fiction typically under 7,500 words, organized around a single dominant impression. Edgar Allan Poe's review essays (1842) articulated the form's compression and unity of effect; modern masters include Hawthorne, Maupassant, Chekhov, Joyce ('The Dead,' Dubliners, 1914), Hemingway, Hurston ('Sweat,' 1926), and Flannery O'Connor. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze how short-fiction compression intensifies craft choices and generates thematic argument.

Flash fiction is very short narrative prose, typically under 1,000 words and sometimes under 100. The form gained prominence in the late 20th century with Lydia Davis, Lorrie Moore, and Hemingway's apocryphal six-word story. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how flash fiction's extreme compression forces dependence on implication, ellipsis, and resonance — every word doing thematic work. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when compression is shown to generate, not merely shorten, meaning.

Fable is a brief narrative, often featuring animals or inanimate objects as characters, that conveys an explicit moral lesson. Aesop's Fables (6th c. BCE) anchor the Western tradition; La Fontaine's Fables (1668–94) refined it in French verse. Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) extends the fable form into political allegory. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how the fable's didactic frame generates ironic distance, allowing critique of human folly through nonhuman protagonists.

Gothic fiction features atmospheric dread, supernatural elements, decaying settings, and psychological terror. Originating with Walpole's The Castle of Otranto (1764), it includes Mary Shelley's Frankenstein (1818), Edgar Allan Poe's tales ('The Fall of the House of Usher,' 1839), Brontë's Wuthering Heights (1847), and modern American Gothic in Faulkner. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how Gothic conventions externalize psychological and social anxieties — gender, sexuality, racial fear, the return of the repressed.

Realism is a literary movement and mode emphasizing accurate depiction of everyday life, ordinary characters, and observable social reality. Flaubert's Madame Bovary (1856), George Eliot's Middlemarch (1872), and Henry James's late novels anchor the European tradition; American realism includes Twain, Howells, and Hurston (Their Eyes Were Watching God, 1937, fusing realism with vernacular Black life). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how realist conventions construct claims about social truth.

Petrarchan (Italian) sonnet, perfected by Francesco Petrarca (Canzoniere, 14th c.), divides its 14 lines into an octave (rhymed abbaabba) and a sestet (cdecde or cdcdcd). The turn (volta) at line 9 typically pivots the argument. Milton, Wordsworth ('London, 1802,' 1802), and Hopkins ('God's Grandeur,' 1877) wrote major Petrarchan sonnets in English. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the form precisely and explain how its octave-sestet logic structures thematic argument.

questions.prompt: Sonnet with three quatrains and couplet.
questions.explanation: Shakespearean (English) sonnet uses three quatrains plus a concluding couplet, rhymed abab cdcd efef gg. The form was adapted from Petrarch by Wyatt and Surrey in the early 16th century and perfected by Shakespeare (Sonnets, 1609). The couplet often delivers an epigrammatic summation or ironic reversal. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how the three-quatrain accumulation plus couplet resolution structures argument, and how the volta (often at line 9 or 13) generates thematic complication.

Volta is the Italian word for 'turn,' the structural pivot of a sonnet. In Petrarchan form the volta occurs at line 9 between octave and sestet; in Shakespearean form it often shifts to line 13 at the couplet. The volta marks a change in argument, perspective, or emotional register. Donne's Holy Sonnet 10 ('Death, be not proud,' 1633), Hopkins's 'God's Grandeur' (1877), and Frost's 'Design' (1936) all hinge on volta. AP Lit Q1 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate the volta precisely and explain how the turn reshapes meaning.

Octave is the eight-line opening unit of a Petrarchan sonnet, conventionally rhymed abbaabba. The octave typically presents a problem, question, or situation that the sestet will respond to. Wordsworth's 'The world is too much with us' (1807) uses the octave to diagnose modern alienation; the sestet rejects rational Christianity for pagan myth. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze how octave-construction sets up the volta and shapes the sestet's interpretive response.

Sestet is the six-line closing unit of a Petrarchan sonnet, typically rhymed cdecde, cdcdcd, or another variant. The sestet responds to or resolves the octave's question or problem. In Milton's 'When I consider how my light is spent' ('On His Blindness,' c. 1652), the sestet delivers Patience's consoling speech. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how the sestet's compressed response generates thematic argument and how its rhyme scheme tightens (or destabilizes) closure.

Elegy is a poem mourning the dead. The English elegiac tradition includes Milton's 'Lycidas' (1637), Gray's 'Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard' (1751), Tennyson's In Memoriam A.H.H. (1850), Whitman's 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' (1865), and Heaney's elegies for his father and brother. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how elegy negotiates grief through formal patterning — repetition, apostrophe, classical reference — and arrives (or refuses to arrive) at consolation.

Ballad is a narrative poem in song-like quatrains, conventionally alternating tetrameter and trimeter rhymed abcb (ballad meter or common meter). Traditional ballads ('Sir Patrick Spens,' 'Lord Randal') were anonymous and oral; literary ballads include Coleridge's 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner' (1798) and Keats's 'La Belle Dame sans Merci' (1819). Dickinson uses ballad meter throughout her hymn-shaped lyrics. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how ballad form's narrative compression generates thematic argument.

Pastoral is a poem (or mode) idealizing rural life, often depicting shepherds in conversation or natural retreat. Theocritus's Idylls (3rd c. BCE) and Virgil's Eclogues (c. 37 BCE) anchor the tradition; Marlowe's 'The Passionate Shepherd to His Love' (1599) and Raleigh's reply animate the Renaissance pastoral debate. Frost's New England lyrics extend it. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how pastoral's idealized frame often generates ironic distance from idealization itself.

Imagery is the use of sensory language — visual, auditory, tactile, olfactory, gustatory, kinesthetic — to create mental images and concrete experience. Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) anchors with the 'cold smell of potato mould, the squelch and slap / Of soggy peat'; Hopkins's 'Pied Beauty' (1877) compresses sensory variety into 'Glory be to God for dappled things.' AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name specific imagery and explain how sensory specificity generates thematic depth.

Visual imagery appeals to sight, the most common sensory channel in poetry. Wallace Stevens's 'Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird' (1923) is sustained visual imagery; Williams's 'The Red Wheelbarrow' (1923) compresses an entire poetic theory into red, white, and rainwater. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who attend not merely to what is seen but to how visual imagery is constructed — angle, distance, color, light — and how those choices generate meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Auditory imagery appeals to hearing — sounds, music, silence, voice. Keats's 'Ode to a Nightingale' (1819) builds on the bird's song; Yeats's 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree' (1890) evokes 'lake water lapping with low sounds by the shore.' AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who attend to specific sonic textures — onomatopoeia, named sounds, contrasts of noise and silence — and explain how auditory imagery generates mood and thematic argument.

Olfactory imagery appeals to smell, often unexpectedly powerful for evoking memory. Heaney's 'cold smell of potato mould' ('Digging,' 1966), Bishop's 'rusted orange' and 'frowsty Provincetown smell' ('At the Fishhouses'), and Whitman's lilac scent in 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' (1865) all anchor olfactory imagery. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name olfactory imagery and explain how smell's involuntary memory triggers thematic depth uniquely.

Kinesthetic imagery appeals to motion, muscle, and bodily sense. Whitman's 'I Sing the Body Electric' (1855) saturates kinesthetic imagery; Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) tracks the father's spade-handle motion across generations. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name kinesthetic imagery precisely and explain how embodied motion generates thematic claim about labor, time, gender, or generational continuity. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when kinesthetic imagery becomes interpretive lever.

Tone shift is a change in the speaker's attitude within a poem, often marking thematic development. Tone may shift from confident to doubting, lamenting to consoled, ironic to earnest. Hopkins's 'Spring and Fall' (1880) modulates from tender to philosophical to elegiac in 15 lines. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate specific tone shifts and explain how diction, syntax, or imagery signals the shift. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when tone shift carries thematic argument, not merely decoration.

Volta — Italian for 'turn' — names the structural pivot of a sonnet at which argument, perspective, or emotion shifts. The Petrarchan volta falls between octave (line 8) and sestet (line 9); the Shakespearean often shifts to the couplet (line 13). Hopkins's 'God's Grandeur' (1877) and Frost's 'Design' (1936) hinge on volta. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the volta precisely and explain how the turn reshapes the poem's interpretive trajectory.

questions.prompt: Volta typically appears at this line.
questions.explanation: Line 9 is the conventional location of the Petrarchan sonnet's volta, marking the transition from octave to sestet. The first eight lines (rhymed abbaabba) typically pose a problem; line 9 pivots into response. Wordsworth's 'London, 1802' (1802) addresses Milton in the octave and pleads for England in the sestet, pivoting precisely at line 9. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate the volta and explain how its placement shapes the poem's logical and thematic architecture.

Line 13 (the start of the couplet) is the conventional location of the Shakespearean sonnet's volta. The three quatrains often develop an argument or descriptive pattern; the closing couplet (lines 13–14) delivers epigrammatic resolution or ironic reversal. Shakespeare's Sonnet 130 ('My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun,' 1609) catalogs anti-blazon imagery, then pivots: 'And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare / As any she belied with false compare.' AP Lit Q1 FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze this turn.

Dramatic monologue is a lyric form in which a single speaker addresses an implied silent listener at a specific moment. Robert Browning perfected it ('My Last Duchess,' 1842; 'Porphyria's Lover,' 1836); T.S. Eliot's 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915) modernized it. The form rewards reading between the lines of the speaker's self-presentation. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who identify dramatic monologue and explain how the implied listener shapes rhetoric and characterization.

Disyllabic feet are two-syllable metrical units — iamb (u /), trochee (/ u), spondee (/ /), pyrrhic (u u). Iambic and trochaic are the most common in English; spondees and pyrrhics typically appear as substitutions rather than dominant patterns. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who scan accurately and identify substitutions — a spondee where an iamb is expected creates emphatic weight; a pyrrhic creates lightness. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when metrical substitution carries interpretive force.

Trisyllabic feet are three-syllable metrical units — anapest (u u /), dactyl (/ u u), amphibrach (u / u). They generate more propulsive rhythms than iambic verse. Anapestic meter drives Byron's 'The Destruction of Sennacherib' (1815); dactylic meter shapes Tennyson's 'The Charge of the Light Brigade' (1854). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify trisyllabic substitutions and explain how the three-syllable rush generates speed, breathlessness, or, in elegies, anguished lament.

Dactyl is a metrical foot of one stressed syllable followed by two unstressed (/ u u), as in 'POE-tic-al.' Dactylic hexameter (six dactyls per line) is the meter of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey (c. 8th c. BCE) and Virgil's Aeneid (c. 19 BCE); Longfellow approximated it in Evangeline (1847). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify dactyls in English verse (where they are rare) and explain how the falling rhythm generates epic gravity, lament, or elevated diction.

Pentameter is a line of five metrical feet, most commonly iambic pentameter in English. The line's length matches a comfortable English breath. Shakespeare's plays and sonnets, Milton's Paradise Lost (1667), Wordsworth's Prelude (1850), and Frost's 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening' (1923) all use iambic pentameter. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the meter precisely and explain how the line's length shapes argument, breath, and rhetorical weight.

Tetrameter is a line of four metrical feet, often iambic. Iambic tetrameter governs Marvell's 'To His Coy Mistress' (1681), Blake's Songs of Innocence and Experience (1789, 1794), and much of Yeats's later work. Ballad meter alternates tetrameter and trimeter. The shorter line generates faster, song-like, or aphoristic rhythms compared with pentameter's measured weight. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how line length — tetrameter vs pentameter — shapes voice, pace, and thematic register.

Novel is a long fictional prose narrative, typically over 40,000 words, organized around a central plot and characters. The English novel emerges with Defoe's Robinson Crusoe (1719), develops through Richardson, Fielding, and Austen, and reaches major nineteenth-century articulation in Dickens, Eliot, and Hardy. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) draw on novels for evidence; the 'List of Suggested Authors' includes Achebe, Atwood, Faulkner, Hurston, Joyce, Morrison, and many others. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) rewards complex argument.

Bildungsroman (German: 'novel of formation') traces a young protagonist's moral, psychological, and social maturation. Goethe's Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (1795–96) named the form; major examples include Dickens's Great Expectations (1861), Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916), Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), and Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958, as anti-bildungsroman). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) frequently use bildungsroman as evidence; sophistication (Row C) rewards critique of what the protagonist matures into.

Epistolary novel is a narrative told through letters, diary entries, or documents. Samuel Richardson's Pamela (1740) and Clarissa (1748) established the form; Frankenstein (Shelley, 1818) is framed epistolarily; Alice Walker's The Color Purple (1982) revives the form for African American voice. Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) closes with a faux scholarly transcript. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B 4 pts) reward students who explain how documentary form constructs authenticity, intimacy, and unreliability simultaneously.

Picaresque novel features a roguish, lower-class protagonist (the pícaro) traveling episodically through a corrupt society. The form originates in 16th-century Spain (Lazarillo de Tormes, 1554); Cervantes's Don Quixote (1605, 1615), though more parody than strict picaresque, is the foundational text. Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) adapts the form. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how picaresque structure — episodic, satirical, marginal — generates social critique.

Dystopian novel imagines a future or alternate society organized around oppression, surveillance, or systemic dehumanization. Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949) and Huxley's Brave New World (1932) anchor the modern tradition; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) and Octavia Butler's Parable of the Sower (1993) extend it. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how dystopian extrapolation satirizes present-tense ideologies. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive complexity.

Narrator is the voice telling the story, distinct from the author. In novels the narrator may be first-person (Huck Finn, Offred), third-person limited (Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God, 1937), third-person omniscient (George Eliot's Middlemarch, 1872), or shift among consciousnesses (Morrison's Beloved, 1987). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the narrator type precisely and explain how narrative voice mediates meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive layering.

First-person narration uses 'I' (or 'we'), restricting the reader to one character's perspective. Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) confines us to Offred's fractured first-person; Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) deploys Huck's vernacular first-person; Hurston frames Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) with Janie's first-person testimony to Pheoby. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who interrogate first-person reliability and explain how voice constructs theme. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through layered reading.

Third-person narration uses 'he,' 'she,' 'they' and stands outside characters. It may be limited (one consciousness) or omniscient (multiple). Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) uses third-person free indirect discourse, drifting in and out of Janie's mind through dialect-saturated narration. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the specific third-person mode and explain how narrative distance generates meaning. Sophistication (Row C) emerges when distance shifts become interpretive evidence.

Omniscient narration grants the storyteller access to all characters' interiorities and to events across time. George Eliot's Middlemarch (1872), Tolstoy's Anna Karenina (1877), and García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) deploy classic omniscient narration. The omniscient narrator can comment philosophically, anticipate later events, and orchestrate sympathy. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how omniscient narration's selective access — which minds to enter and when — constructs theme and irony.

Stream of consciousness is a narrative technique rendering the unstructured flow of a character's mind — sensations, memories, half-formed thoughts. Joyce perfects it in Ulysses (1922, especially Molly Bloom's closing monologue); Woolf deploys it in Mrs Dalloway (1925) and To the Lighthouse (1927); Faulkner uses it across The Sound and the Fury (1929). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name stream of consciousness precisely and explain how syntactic fragmentation generates psychological realism and thematic depth.

Symbol is a concrete object or image representing an abstract idea. The chokecherry tree on Sethe's back in Morrison's Beloved (1987), the lighthouse in Woolf's To the Lighthouse (1927), and the green light in Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby (1925) all carry symbolic weight beyond their literal presence. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify specific symbols and trace their interpretive resonance. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when symbol's meaning shifts across the work, generating thematic complexity.

Motif is a recurring symbolic element — image, phrase, situation — that gathers thematic weight through repetition. The recurring image of buried bodies in Faulkner's As I Lay Dying (1930), the references to disease in Hamlet (Shakespeare, 1601), and the rememory motif in Morrison's Beloved (1987) all build cumulative meaning. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish motif from symbol (motif emphasizes recurrence) and trace how repetition generates thematic argument across the novel's length.

Allegory is an extended narrative in which characters, events, and settings systematically represent abstract concepts. Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress (1678) allegorizes Christian salvation; Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) allegorizes the Russian Revolution; Spenser's The Faerie Queene (1590–96) is encyclopedic Reformation-era political-religious allegory. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who distinguish allegory from symbolism (allegory is systematic, sustained) and explain how allegorical mapping constructs thematic argument.

Archetype is a universal symbol or character type recurring across cultures and centuries — hero, mentor, shadow, threshold guardian, journey, fall, paradise lost. Carl Jung (The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious, 1959) theorized archetypes as patterns of the collective unconscious; Northrop Frye (Anatomy of Criticism, 1957) systematized literary archetypes by season and genre. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how an author deploys, complicates, or subverts archetypal patterns to generate thematic argument.

National symbol is an object, place, or figure representing a nation's identity. The eagle, the flag, and the frontier function as American national symbols; the rose for England; the kola nut, palm wine, and yam for Igbo Nigeria in Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958). AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how novels deploy national symbols — embracing, critiquing, or interrogating them — and how such symbols carry the weight of historical and political argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through critique.

Theme is the underlying meaning or insight a work conveys. In Morrison's Beloved (1987) one theme is the inescapable weight of slavery's afterlife; in Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) it is the fragility of women's rights under fundamentalist patriarchy. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row A 1 pt, Row B 4 pts, Row C 1 pt) require students to argue interpretive claims about theme — not merely identify them — using specific textual evidence tied to commentary. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive complexity.

Coming-of-age (Bildungsroman) traces a young protagonist's maturation. Goethe's Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (1795–96) names the form; major examples include Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916), Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), and Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) as anti-bildungsroman in which Okonkwo refuses maturation. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who select coming-of-age works and interrogate what the protagonist matures into and at what cost.

Social criticism is a thematic mode exposing the injustices, inequalities, or hypocrisies of a given society. Dickens's Oliver Twist (1838) and Bleak House (1853) critique Victorian poverty and bureaucracy; Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) critiques gender and racial constraints; Morrison's Beloved (1987) critiques the lived afterlife of American slavery. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how authorial choices construct social critique. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when critique gains interpretive depth.

Person-versus-nature is a thematic conflict in which character struggles against the natural environment as antagonist or as condition. Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) folds the Congo's wilderness into psychological terrain; Hemingway's The Old Man and the Sea (1952) and McCarthy's The Road (2006) anchor the tradition. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how nature operates symbolically — indifferent, threatening, regenerative — and carries thematic argument about civilization, mortality, or human limits.

questions.prompt: Theme of fate versus free will.
questions.explanation: Determinism is the philosophical position that events, including human choices, are causally determined by prior conditions. In Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles (1891) Tess's fate is determined by class, gender, and 'President of the Immortals'; in Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) Gilead's structure determines Offred's options. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue the work's stance on determinism versus free will using specific evidence. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through complex synthesis.

Style is the distinctive manner in which an author uses language — diction, syntax, sentence rhythm, figurative density, narrative distance. Hemingway's minimalism contrasts Faulkner's maximalism; Hurston's vernacular saturation contrasts Henry James's elaborate Latinate syntax.
