AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze style as collection of authorial choices generating meaning, not as ornament. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when stylistic analysis ties specific patterns to thematic argument and historical-cultural context.

Minimalist style strips prose of ornament, leaving short sentences, plain diction, and implication. Hemingway's iceberg theory (Death in the Afternoon, 1932) proposed that 'the dignity of movement of an iceberg is due to only one-eighth of it being above water'; his stories ('Hills Like White Elephants,' 1927) embody it. Raymond Carver's stories continue it. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how minimalist style forces dependence on subtext, ellipsis, and implication.

Maximalist style amplifies prose with long sentences, dense syntax, recursive embedding, and elaborate figuration. Faulkner's The Sound and the Fury (1929) and Absalom, Absalom! (1936) push English syntax to its limits, embedding clauses within clauses to mirror consciousness folding back on itself. García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) deploys lush figurative density. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how maximalist style enacts thematic concerns of history, memory, and complexity.

Stream of consciousness is a narrative technique rendering the unstructured flow of a character's interiority. Joyce perfects it in Ulysses (1922), especially Molly Bloom's closing monologue ('yes I said yes I will Yes'). Woolf's Mrs Dalloway (1925) deploys it more lyrically; Faulkner's The Sound and the Fury (1929) varies it across multiple consciousnesses. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name stream of consciousness precisely and explain how syntactic fragmentation generates psychological realism and modernist theme.

Regional dialect is the rendering of a specific community's speech patterns in narrative. Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) uses multiple Mississippi River dialects; Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) renders rural Florida Black vernacular as primary narrative medium, refusing standard English's hierarchy. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how dialect generates authenticity, social critique, and resistance to linguistic standardization. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered cultural reading.

Modernism is the literary movement (roughly 1890–1945) responding to World War I, urbanization, secularization, and the collapse of nineteenth-century certainties. Major modernists include T.S. Eliot ('Prufrock,' 1915; The Waste Land, 1922), Joyce (Dubliners 1914, Ulysses 1922), Woolf, Faulkner, Pound, Stevens, H.D. and Hemingway. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who anchor modernist analysis in specific historical conditions and explain how form — fragmentation, allusion, stream of consciousness — enacts thematic argument.

Fragmentation is the modernist technique of breaking narrative, syntax, and image into discontinuous pieces, mirroring the perceived shattering of cultural coherence after WWI. Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) is built on fragmentation — quoted languages, broken images, disjunct voices. Faulkner's The Sound and the Fury (1929) fragments through multiple consciousnesses. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis and Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how fragmentation enacts the modernist diagnosis that 'these fragments I have shored against my ruins.'

Stream of consciousness is the modernist narrative technique rendering the unstructured flow of a character's mind — sensations, memories, associations. Joyce's Ulysses (1922, Molly's closing monologue) and Woolf's Mrs Dalloway (1925) anchor the form. The technique was theorized by William James (The Principles of Psychology, 1890) and adopted as literary method. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how syntactic fragmentation and free association generate psychological realism and modernist theme.

Skepticism in modernism is the questioning of inherited authorities — religion, empire, gender norms, Victorian moral confidence. Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) drowns in skepticism; Joyce's Dubliners (1914) skewers Irish Catholic and nationalist pieties; Woolf's essays interrogate patriarchy. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how modernist skepticism — not mere doubt but interrogation of meaning's grounds — generates formal innovation. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered cultural-historical reading.

Alienation is the modernist sense of estrangement from self, community, history, and meaning. Kafka's The Metamorphosis (1915) literalizes alienation as Gregor Samsa awakens transformed; Camus's The Stranger (1942) and The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) theorize it as the absurd. Eliot's J. Alfred Prufrock (1915) embodies modernist alienation through paralyzing self-consciousness. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how alienation generates modernist form and theme.

Postmodernism is the literary and cultural movement following modernism (roughly 1945–present), characterized by skepticism toward grand narratives, playful self-awareness, intertextuality, and genre-blending. Major postmodernists include Pynchon (Gravity's Rainbow, 1973), Barthelme, Calvino (If on a winter's night a traveler, 1979), Borges (Ficciones, 1944), DeLillo, and Atwood. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who treat postmodernism not as mere style but as response to specific historical conditions of postwar disillusionment.

Metafiction is postmodern fiction that draws attention to its own status as fiction. Calvino's If on a winter's night a traveler (1979) addresses 'You, the Reader' directly; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) ends with a faux academic transcript exposing Offred's narrative as constructed text. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B 4 pts, Row C 1 pt) reward students who explain how metafiction's reflexivity generates thematic argument — about the constructed nature of identity, history, and narrative itself.

Genre hybridity is the postmodern blending of literary modes — high and low, realism and fantasy, fiction and essay, novel and detective story. Borges's Ficciones (1944) mixes scholarly essay and short story; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) fuses dystopia, autobiography, and faux-history. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how genre hybridity destabilizes inherited categories and generates thematic critique of fixed forms. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Intertextuality is the postmodern web of references and dependencies between texts — quotation, allusion, parody, rewriting. Julia Kristeva (Word, Dialogue, and Novel, 1966) coined the term; Roland Barthes (S/Z, 1970; 'The Death of the Author,' 1967) theorized text as 'a tissue of quotations.' Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) prefigures intertextuality; postmodern fiction makes it constitutive. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace intertextual references and explain how they generate meaning.

Skepticism toward metanarratives is Jean-François Lyotard's term (The Postmodern Condition, 1979) for the postmodern refusal of universal explanatory stories — Christian salvation, Enlightenment progress, Marxist liberation. Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) interrogates the Christian metanarrative of patriarchal authority; Morrison's Beloved (1987) refuses the metanarrative of American progress. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how skepticism toward metanarratives generates formal and thematic argument.

Character revelation is the gradual disclosure of character traits, motives, and depths through action, dialogue, and narration. Faulkner's 'A Rose for Emily' (1930) reveals Emily Grierson through fragmented chronology, withholding the climactic discovery until the final paragraph. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace specific revelations and explain how their placement, withholding, and final disclosure generate thematic meaning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive layering.

Epiphany is Joyce's term (Stephen Hero, posthumous; perfected in Dubliners, 1914) for a sudden spiritual or psychological revelation — 'a sudden spiritual manifestation, whether in the vulgarity of speech or of gesture or in a memorable phase of the mind itself.' Gabriel Conroy's epiphany at the close of 'The Dead' (1914) is the paradigmatic instance. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name epiphany precisely and explain how its placement and content generate thematic argument.

Interior monologue is the rendering of a character's thoughts in continuous prose, typically signaled by stylistic shift from external narration. Joyce's Ulysses (1922) develops it across multiple consciousnesses; Woolf's To the Lighthouse (1927) deploys it through free indirect discourse. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name the technique precisely and explain how syntactic markers — fragments, association, internal address — generate psychological realism and thematic depth. Sophistication (Row C) emerges through analytic precision.

Setting as character is the literary technique in which place functions actively — shaping action, embodying theme, generating meaning beyond backdrop. The Congo River in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) is geography and psychology at once; 124 Bluestone Road in Morrison's Beloved (1987) is haunted house and haunted history. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat setting as active participant in theme. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when setting's agency carries interpretive argument.

Pathetic fallacy is John Ruskin's term (Modern Painters III, 1856) for landscape that mirrors human emotion — the storm raging when Lear rages on the heath (Shakespeare, King Lear, 1606), the gathering darkness over the moors in Brontë's Wuthering Heights (1847), the oppressive Spanish summer in Hemingway. Heaney's 'Storm on the Island' deploys it modernly. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name pathetic fallacy precisely and explain how the device fuses inner and outer worlds, generating thematic depth.

City setting in modern fiction often functions as character and theme — alienation, anonymity, speed, possibility. Joyce's Dublin in Dubliners (1914) is character; Eliot's London in The Waste Land (1922) is fragmented modernity; Atwood's reimagined Cambridge in The Handmaid's Tale (1985) is repurposed for theocratic surveillance. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat urban setting as active site of theme — class, race, gender, isolation — and explain how city specificity generates argument.

Country setting in fiction often functions as pastoral idealization, cultural authenticity, or constraining tradition. Hurston's Eatonville and the Everglades in Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) anchor Janie's self-discovery; Achebe's Umuofia in Things Fall Apart (1958) is precolonial Igbo community; García Márquez's Macondo in One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) is magical Latin American village. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how rural setting generates thematic argument — about tradition, community, ecological knowledge.

Regionalism is the literary commitment to a specific geographic and cultural location's particularities. Faulkner's invented Yoknapatawpha County (Mississippi) anchors Absalom, Absalom! (1936), The Sound and the Fury (1929), and 'A Rose for Emily' (1930); Hurston's Florida Black communities anchor her fiction; Eudora Welty's Mississippi anchors hers. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how regional specificity grounds universal themes and refuses placeless abstraction.

Diction is word choice — the lexical register, formality, abstraction, sound, and connotation an author selects. Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) mixes Anglo-Saxon physicality ('squelch,' 'slap') with abstract Latinate reflection; Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) uses nursery-rhyme diction for adult fury. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis and Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name specific diction patterns and explain their thematic effect. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when diction becomes interpretive evidence rather than 'fancy words.'

Formal diction is elevated, often Latinate, syntactically careful word choice associated with academic, ceremonial, or aristocratic registers. Milton's Paradise Lost (1667) sustains formal Latinate diction; Henry James's late novels deploy formal English. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify formal diction and explain how its register generates authority, distance, or ironic contrast with subject matter. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when formal diction's stiffness is shown to generate thematic critique.

Colloquial diction is everyday, casual, conversational word choice — contractions, regional expressions, idioms. Twain's Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) is sustained colloquial diction in Huck's first-person; Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) renders rural Florida Black vernacular as primary narrative medium. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how colloquial diction generates authenticity, cultural specificity, or resistance to standardized literary English.

Jargon is specialized vocabulary of a particular profession, discipline, or community — legal, medical, scientific, military, theological. Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) deploys religious-bureaucratic Gilead jargon ('Particicution,' 'Salvaging,' 'Econowife') to expose ideological construction of language. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify jargon precisely and explain how specialized lexicon generates thematic argument — about power, expertise, exclusion, or ideological capture.

Archaic diction is the use of old-fashioned words, syntax, or grammatical forms — 'thou,' 'thee,' inverted clauses, dropped articles. Keats's 'La Belle Dame sans Merci' (1819) deploys archaic medievalism; Tennyson's Idylls of the King (1859–85) sustains Arthurian archaisms. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name archaic diction precisely and explain how its evocation of past registers generates thematic argument — about tradition, mythic resonance, or ironic distance.

Sestina is a six-stanza poem of six unrhymed lines plus a three-line envoi (39 lines total), in which six end-words rotate through a fixed permutation across the stanzas. Originated by Provençal troubadour Arnaut Daniel (12th c.); revived in English by Sidney, Auden, Pound, and Elizabeth Bishop ('Sestina,' 'A Miracle for Breakfast'). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify sestina precisely and explain how the obsessive return of six end-words generates thematic argument about memory, compulsion, or constraint.

Cycle (or sequence) is a sustained series of related poems read together as unified work. Shakespeare's Sonnets (1609) form a cycle; Whitman's Leaves of Grass (1855, expanded throughout his life) is cyclic; Berryman's Dream Songs (1964, 1968), Plath's Ariel (1965), and Rita Dove's Thomas and Beulah (1986) anchor twentieth-century cycles. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who treat cycle as architectural choice generating accumulating meaning through repetition, variation, and progression.

Epic is a long narrative poem of elevated style centered on a hero whose actions carry national or civilizational consequence. Homer's Iliad and Odyssey (c. 8th c. BCE) anchor the Western epic; Virgil's Aeneid (c. 19 BCE), Milton's Paradise Lost (1667), and Derek Walcott's Omeros (1990) extend it. Epic conventions include invocation of the muse, in medias res opening, epic similes, and divine intervention. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who explain how epic conventions construct thematic argument.

Speaker is the persona voicing a poem, distinct from the poet. Browning's dramatic monologues — 'My Last Duchess' (1842), 'Porphyria's Lover' (1836) — pioneered the technique of constructing a speaker whose self-revelation exceeds his self-knowledge. Eliot's J. Alfred Prufrock (1915) speaks neuroses he barely owns; Plath's 'Lady Lazarus' (1962) constructs a speaker performing her own resurrection. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish speaker from poet and analyze how the speaker's voice generates meaning.

Auditor is the implied listener within a poem — the silent recipient of the speaker's address. Browning's dramatic monologues construct distinct auditors: the visiting envoy in 'My Last Duchess' (1842), the doctors in 'Andrea del Sarto' (1855). Eliot's 'Prufrock' (1915) addresses 'you and I,' an ambiguous auditor possibly a divided self. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify the auditor precisely and explain how the implied listener shapes rhetoric, omission, and characterization.

Dramatic monologue is a poem in which a single speaker addresses an implied silent listener at a specific dramatic moment. Robert Browning perfected the form — 'My Last Duchess' (1842) reveals the Duke of Ferrara through what he says to the marriage envoy and, more damningly, what he reveals without intending. Tennyson's 'Ulysses' (1842) and Eliot's 'Prufrock' (1915) anchor the modern tradition. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who identify dramatic monologue and explain how the silent auditor shapes meaning.

Apostrophe is direct address to an absent person, abstract concept, or nonhuman entity. Shelley's 'Ode to the West Wind' (1820) ('O wild West Wind, thou breath of Autumn's being'), Keats's 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' (1819) ('Thou still unravish'd bride of quietness'), and Donne's Holy Sonnet 10 ('Death, be not proud,' 1633) all deploy apostrophe. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how apostrophe constructs speaker stance — invocation, defiance, mourning — and generates thematic argument.

Apostrophe to the dead is a specialized form of apostrophe addressed to a deceased person, common in elegy. Milton's 'Lycidas' (1637) addresses the dead Edward King; Tennyson's In Memoriam A.H.H. (1850) addresses Arthur Hallam across 133 sections; Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) addresses her dead father in twisted apostrophe. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name apostrophe to the dead precisely and explain how the impossibility of response generates elegiac tension, accusation, or unresolved grief.

Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in nearby words. Old English verse (Beowulf, c. 8th–11th c.) was structured on alliterative meter; Hopkins revived alliterative density ('Glory be to God for dappled things,' 'Pied Beauty,' 1877); Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) deploys it ('squelch and slap of soggy peat'). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name alliteration precisely and explain how the device generates emphasis, mood, or thematic linkage — not merely list instances.

Assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds in nearby words regardless of consonants. Dickinson's poems deploy assonance ('A narrow Fellow in the Grass') for internal music without rhyme; Yeats's 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree' (1890) deploys long-i and long-o assonance to slow lines. Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) uses driving short-a assonance. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name assonance precisely and explain how vowel music generates mood, slowness, or harmonic resonance.

Consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds at the end (or interior) of words, often without matching vowels. Hopkins's 'pitched past pitch of grief' (sonnet 'No worst, there is none,' 1885) deploys consonance to bind tonal weight; Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) repeats hard 'k' and 't' sounds. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish consonance from alliteration (positional) and from assonance (vowels) and explain how the device generates rhythmic and thematic effect.

Slant rhyme (also called near rhyme, half rhyme, or oblique rhyme) is approximate rhyme — matched consonants but mismatched vowels, or vice versa. Emily Dickinson uses slant rhyme throughout her work ('soul' / 'all,' 'door' / 'are' in #479); Yeats deploys it in his late poems; Heaney uses it densely. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name slant rhyme precisely and explain how its near-but-not-quite resolution generates dissonance, suspended meaning, or refusal of formal closure.

Onomatopoeia is the use of words that imitate sounds — 'buzz,' 'clang,' 'murmur,' 'splash,' 'hiss.' Tennyson's 'The moan of doves in immemorial elms, / And murmuring of innumerable bees' ('Come Down, O Maid,' 1847) is a textbook line; Hopkins's sprung rhythm deploys onomatopoeia to fuse sound and meaning. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify onomatopoeia and explain how sonic imitation reinforces thematic content rather than merely decorating.

Motif is a recurring image, symbol, or situation that gathers meaning through repetition across a poem or cycle. Yeats's recurring images of gyres, swans, and falcons across his Collected Poems; Eliot's recurring water images in The Waste Land (1922); Plath's recurring images of vampires, statues, and trains in Ariel (1965). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace motif precisely and explain how repetition generates cumulative thematic argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Symbol is a concrete image representing an abstract idea. Yeats's gyre symbolizes historical cycle ('The Second Coming,' 1920); Frost's road symbolizes choice ('The Road Not Taken,' 1916); Plath's 'fig tree' symbolizes paralyzing choice (The Bell Jar, 1963). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify specific symbols and trace their interpretive resonance. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when a symbol's meaning is shown to shift, generating thematic complexity.

Allusion is reference to another text, person, event, or work of art. Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) is built on dense allusion — Dante, Shakespeare, Augustine, Frazer's Golden Bough, Hindu Upanishads. Yeats alludes to Irish mythology; Plath alludes to Holocaust imagery in 'Daddy' (1962). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify allusion precisely and explain how the referenced text deepens or complicates the poem's argument. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive layering.

Imagistic clusters are groups of related images that recur and develop across a poem or sequence. Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) clusters water (drowning, baptism, drought), urban images (London Bridge, the Thames, City clerks), and broken images. Plath's Ariel (1965) clusters vampires, beekeeping, color images of red and white. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who trace imagistic clusters and explain how grouped images generate cumulative thematic argument.

Central image is the single dominant image around which a poem's meaning organizes. Frost's birch trees ('Birches,' 1916) anchor the entire poem's meditation on imagination and reality; Plath's beehive in 'The Arrival of the Bee Box' (1962) anchors her poem of constraint and agency. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify the central image and explain how its specific construction — physical features, sensory detail, symbolic resonance — generates the poem's thematic argument.

Tone is the speaker's attitude toward subject, audience, or self, conveyed through diction, syntax, and imagery. Hopkins's 'Spring and Fall' (1880) shifts from tender to elegiac to philosophical in 15 lines. Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) carries a tone simultaneously enraged and incantatory. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify tone precisely with specific adjectives — sardonic, reverent, anguished, defiant — and explain how authorial choices construct it. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through tone-shift analysis.

Elegiac tone is the mournful, lamenting attitude characteristic of elegy. Tennyson's In Memoriam A.H.H. (1850), Whitman's 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' (1865), and Heaney's 'Mid-Term Break' all sustain elegiac tone. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name elegiac tone precisely and explain how diction (specific abstractions of loss), syntax (long sustained sentences, repetition, apostrophe), and imagery (autumn, evening, falling) construct mourning. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through interpretive nuance.

Ironic tone is the speaker's stance of doubleness — apparent meaning differing from real meaning, often with critical or skeptical edge. Eliot's J. Alfred Prufrock (1915) speaks in ironic tone, his self-aggrandizing diction undercut by self-deprecating qualification. Larkin's poems sustain ironic tone. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify ironic tone and explain how the gap between literal statement and intended meaning generates thematic critique. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Reverent tone is the attitude of awe, respect, or worshipful contemplation. Hopkins's 'Pied Beauty' (1877) and 'God's Grandeur' (1877) sustain reverent tone toward divine creation; Whitman's 'When I Heard the Learn'd Astronomer' (1865) shifts to reverent tone before 'the perfect silence at the stars.' AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name reverent tone precisely and explain how elevated diction, syntactic gravity, and imagery of grandeur construct attitude. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through nuance.

Objective tone is the attitude of detached observation — minimal emotional inflection, factual diction, distance from subject. William Carlos Williams's 'The Red Wheelbarrow' (1923), Wallace Stevens's 'The Snow Man' (1921), and much Imagist poetry sustain objective tone. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name objective tone precisely and explain how restraint generates thematic argument — about modernist refusal of Victorian sentimentality, about the meaning-bearing capacity of bare image.

questions.prompt: First FRQ on AP Lit exam.
questions.explanation: FRQ 1 is the poetry-analysis free-response question on the AP English Literature exam. Students are given one or two short poems (typically 20–50 lines) and asked to analyze how the poet uses literary elements — imagery, diction, structure, tone, figurative language — to convey meaning. The 6-point rubric awards Row A (1 pt: defensible thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary), and Row C (1 pt: sophistication). Recent passages have included poems by Frost, Bishop, Heaney, Levertov, and Donne.

FRQ 1 (and all three AP Lit FRQs) is allotted 40 minutes of writing time. The total Section II essay time is 2 hours for three FRQs. Students should budget roughly 8–10 minutes for annotation and planning, 25–30 minutes for drafting, and 2–5 minutes for revision. The 6-point rubric rewards focused, well-supported analysis over comprehensive coverage; students who attempt to address every literary device produce shallow essays. Strong essays anchor on 2–3 specific devices generating thematic argument tied to the poem's central concern.

FRQ 1's analytical focus is on the author's craft choices and their effect on meaning — not paraphrase, not biographical speculation, not vague appreciation. Students must explain how specific authorial decisions (this enjambment, this diction shift, this image cluster) generate the poem's interpretive significance. The 6-point rubric's Row B (4 pts) explicitly rewards evidence-plus-commentary linkages: a quoted line followed by reasoned analysis of why the choice matters. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned by complex interpretation transcending obvious readings.

FRQ 1 typically presents one short poem of 20–50 lines, occasionally a brief paired set. Recent prompts have used Frost's 'Acquainted with the Night' (1928), Heaney's 'Mid-Term Break' (1966), Bishop's 'The Fish' (1946), and Larkin's late lyrics. Students should expect a single self-contained poem rather than an excerpt from a longer work. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) demands close engagement with specific textual evidence — direct quotation, paraphrase with line citation — rather than vague gestures toward the whole poem.

FRQ scores range from 0 to 6 on each question. The 6-point rubric (current as of the 2019 redesign) distributes points: Row A (1 pt: defensible thesis with a line of reasoning); Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary, distributed across 1–4 pts depending on specificity and analytical depth); Row C (1 pt: sophistication — complex literary argument, broad interpretive frame, or vivid prose). All three FRQs use identical rubric architecture, allowing students to internalize one analytical framework applicable across poetry, prose, and literary argument.

questions.prompt: Second FRQ on AP Lit exam.
questions.explanation: FRQ 2 is the prose-analysis free-response question on the AP English Literature exam. Students are given an excerpt of fiction or drama (typically 500–800 words, often a complete scene or short chapter) and asked to analyze how the author uses literary elements — characterization, narrative voice, syntax, imagery, dialogue — to convey meaning. The 6-point rubric awards Row A (1 pt: thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary), Row C (1 pt: sophistication). Recent passages have used Faulkner, Atwood, Conrad, and Hurston.

FRQ 2 prose passages are drawn from fiction or drama — novels, short stories, plays. Recent excerpts have come from Faulkner's As I Lay Dying (1930), Atwood's Surfacing (1972), Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899), Edith Wharton's The Age of Innocence (1920), and various contemporary novels. Drama excerpts have included scenes from Tennessee Williams's The Glass Menagerie (1944) and August Wilson's Fences (1985). The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards close engagement with the excerpt's specific craft choices.

FRQ 2's analytical focus is on the writer's craft — how authorial choices shape meaning. The prompt typically asks students to analyze a specific aspect (characterization, conflict, narrative voice, setting, tone) and trace its construction across the excerpt. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) demands evidence-plus-commentary: a quoted phrase or detail followed by reasoned analysis. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned when interpretation moves beyond identification into complex argument about the excerpt's interpretive depth.

FRQ 2 (like all AP Lit FRQs) is allotted 40 minutes within the 2-hour Section II essay window. Students should budget 8–10 minutes for annotation and planning, 25–30 minutes for drafting, and 2–5 minutes for revision. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards focused depth over breadth: strong essays anchor on 2–3 specific craft choices and trace them carefully rather than cataloguing every device the excerpt contains. The sophistication point (Row C, 1 pt) requires complex interpretation, not coverage.

FRQ 2 commonly tests identification and analysis of literary devices in prose — characterization techniques (direct vs indirect), narrative voice (first vs third, limited vs omniscient, reliable vs unreliable), figurative language (metaphor, simile, personification), syntax (sentence length, fragmentation, parallelism), and structure (flashback, frame narrative, in medias res). The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards essays that move from naming devices to analyzing their interpretive function. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) requires complex argument.

questions.prompt: Third FRQ on AP Lit exam.
questions.explanation: FRQ 3 is the literary-argument free-response question on the AP English Literature exam. Students are given a thematic or interpretive prompt and asked to choose a full-length work (novel or play) — from the prompt's suggested list or their own knowledge — and defend a thesis about how the work develops the theme. The 6-point rubric awards Row A (1 pt: defensible thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary from the chosen work), Row C (1 pt: sophistication). FRQ 3 rewards students who have read deeply.

FRQ 3 evidence is drawn from a complete novel or play the student has read — not from a passage the exam provides. Students must rely on memory of specific scenes, characters, lines, and authorial choices. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards essays that cite specific textual moments with reasoned analysis. Vague summary or plot rehearsal fails the rubric. Students should arrive at the exam with 3–5 thoroughly studied works — typically one tragedy, one novel of social critique, one bildungsroman, one modernist work, one work of color.

questions.prompt: List of recommended works given on exam.
questions.explanation: FRQ 3 provides a Suggested Authors List of approximately 40 works that students may choose from. The list rotates each exam and includes works such as Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958), Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985), Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899), Faulkner's As I Lay Dying (1930), Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), Morrison's Beloved (1987), Shakespeare's tragedies (Hamlet 1601, Macbeth 1606, Othello 1604, King Lear 1606), and many others. Students may write on a work not on the list if it is of comparable literary merit.

FRQ 3 (like all three AP Lit FRQs) is allotted 40 minutes within the 2-hour Section II essay window. Students should budget 8–10 minutes for thesis construction and outlining, 25–30 minutes for drafting, and 2–5 minutes for revision. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards essays that defend a specific interpretive thesis using carefully chosen evidence. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) requires complex argument that transcends obvious readings — interrogating the work's contradictions, ironies, or ambiguities rather than confirming them.

FRQ 3 requires students to defend an interpretation of a chosen work using specific evidence. The 6-point rubric prizes argument over summary: Row A (1 pt) rewards a defensible thesis with a clear line of reasoning; Row B (4 pts) rewards evidence-plus-commentary linkages; Row C (1 pt: sophistication) rewards complex interpretation, broad analytical frame, or vivid prose. Common failure modes include plot summary disguised as analysis, vague generalities without textual anchor, and theses that merely restate the prompt without staking a defensible interpretive claim.

Aristotle (384–322 BCE) defined tragedy in the Poetics (335 BCE) as 'an imitation of an action that is serious and complete, of a certain magnitude... effecting through pity and fear the catharsis of such emotions.' He identified six elements (plot, character, thought, diction, music, spectacle) and key terms (hamartia, peripeteia, anagnorisis, catharsis). AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis and Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who anchor tragic analysis in Aristotelian terms while interrogating, not just applying, the framework.

Hamartia is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the tragic protagonist's error or flaw that precipitates catastrophe — often translated 'tragic flaw' but better rendered 'missing the mark.' Oedipus's hubris (Sophocles, Oedipus Rex, c. 429 BCE) and Macbeth's ambition (Shakespeare, 1606) are classic hamartiai. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name hamartia precisely and trace its operation through specific scenes. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when hamartia is interrogated as moral failure or structural inevitability.

Catharsis is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the audience's purgation of pity and fear through witnessing tragedy. The exact mechanism — medical purge, emotional release, intellectual clarification — has been debated since the Renaissance. Hamlet's, Othello's, and Lear's deaths in Shakespeare's tragedies (1601, 1604, 1606) aim at catharsis. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who deploy catharsis as interpretive frame. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned by interrogating what catharsis achieves.

Soliloquy is a dramatic convention in which a character, alone on stage, speaks thoughts aloud to the audience. Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' (Hamlet 3.1, 1601) is the most famous instance; Macbeth's 'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow' (Macbeth 5.5, 1606) and Iago's confessional soliloquies in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) rank alongside. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze soliloquy as carefully crafted self-revelation — not merely 'inner thought' — and explain how its rhetoric constructs character and theme.

Tragicomedy blends tragic and comic elements, typically with serious threats resolved nontragically. Shakespeare's late 'problem plays' — Measure for Measure (1604), All's Well That Ends Well (c. 1604), and the romances Pericles, The Winter's Tale (1611), Cymbeline, and The Tempest (1611) — bend tragic momentum toward reconciliation. Beckett's Waiting for Godot (1953) modernized the form. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who argue how tragicomedy's hybrid generates interpretive complexity. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Motif is a recurring symbolic element — image, phrase, situation — that gathers thematic weight through repetition. Hamlet's references to disease and decay (Shakespeare, 1601), the chokecherry tree and rememory in Morrison's Beloved (1987), and the bee imagery in Plath's Ariel (1965) generate cumulative meaning. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis and Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish motif from symbol (motif emphasizes recurrence) and trace how repetition generates interpretive argument across a work's length.

Symbol is a concrete object or image representing an abstract idea. Hawthorne's scarlet letter A (The Scarlet Letter, 1850), Conrad's Congo River (Heart of Darkness, 1899), Morrison's chokecherry tree on Sethe's back (Beloved, 1987), and Plath's bell jar (The Bell Jar, 1963) all carry symbolic weight beyond their literal presence. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify specific symbols and trace their interpretive resonance. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges when symbol's meaning shifts.

Narrator is the voice telling a story, distinct from the author. Marlow in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) is a framed first-person narrator nested inside an unnamed outer narrator; Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) uses third-person free indirect discourse. Atwood's Offred (1985) and Twain's Huck Finn (1885) anchor first-person traditions. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish narrator from author and explain how narrative voice mediates meaning.

Tone is the author's (or speaker's) attitude toward subject, audience, or self, conveyed through diction, syntax, and imagery. Hopkins's 'Spring and Fall' (1880) modulates from tender to elegiac to philosophical; Plath's 'Daddy' (1962) carries a tone simultaneously enraged and incantatory; Atwood's Offred narrates in tone of fragmented witness. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis and Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify tone precisely and explain how authorial choices construct it. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through tone-shift analysis.

Protagonist is the central character whose journey a narrative traces. Joyce's Gabriel Conroy in 'The Dead' (Dubliners, 1914), Hurston's Janie Crawford in Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937), Achebe's Okonkwo in Things Fall Apart (1958), and Morrison's Sethe in Beloved (1987) all anchor major novels. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric: Row A thesis 1 pt, Row B evidence and commentary 4 pts, Row C sophistication 1 pt) reward students who select protagonists whose complexity supports defensible interpretive claims about theme.

Antagonist embodies the opposing force, person, or system that resists the protagonist. In Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) the Republic of Gilead itself is the antagonist of Offred; in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) Kurtz is antagonist and dark double for Marlow; in Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) the colonial District Commissioner culminates Okonkwo's destruction. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat antagonist as instrument of theme — not mere villain — and earn sophistication (Row C) through layered reading.

Setting is the time and place of a narrative, including historical, cultural, and physical context. Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is inseparable from rural Florida and Eatonville; Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) is inseparable from Gilead's repurposed Cambridge; García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) cannot be extracted from Macondo. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who treat setting as active participant in theme rather than backdrop.

Narrator is the voice telling a story, distinct from the author. Marlow in Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) is a framed first-person narrator inside an unnamed outer narrator; Offred in Atwood's The Handmaid's Tale (1985) is fractured first-person; Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) uses third-person free indirect discourse drifting in and out of Janie's consciousness. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name narrator type precisely and explain how voice mediates meaning.

Theme is the underlying meaning a work conveys, distinct from subject. Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) has the subject of colonialism but the theme of how rigid masculinity compounds catastrophe; Morrison's Beloved (1987) has the subject of slavery's afterlife and the theme of memory's burden. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric: Row A 1 pt for thesis, Row B 4 pts for evidence and commentary, Row C 1 pt for sophistication) require interpretive argument about theme, not mere identification.

Sonnet is a 14-line lyric in iambic pentameter, originated by Giacomo da Lentini in 13th-century Sicily, perfected by Petrarch (Canzoniere, 14th c.), and naturalized in English by Wyatt, Surrey, and Shakespeare (Sonnets, 1609). Petrarchan and Shakespearean forms both rotate on a structural turn (volta). Donne's Holy Sonnets (1633), Hopkins's terrible sonnets, and Heaney's Glanmore Sonnets extend the tradition. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who explain how sonnet form generates meaning.

Iamb is a metrical foot of one unstressed syllable followed by one stressed (u /), as in 'to-DAY.' It is the dominant foot of English verse because English speech rhythm naturally falls into iambic patterns. Shakespeare's blank verse, Donne's Holy Sonnets, Frost's 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening' (1923), and Heaney's lyrics use iambs. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify iambic patterns and explain how variations (trochaic inversion, spondee substitution) generate emphasis and meaning.

Volta is the Italian word for 'turn,' the structural pivot of a sonnet. In Petrarchan form (octave-sestet, rhymed abbaabba cdecde) the volta falls at line 9; in Shakespearean form (three quatrains plus couplet, rhymed abab cdcd efef gg) it often shifts to line 13. Donne's Holy Sonnet 10 ('Death, be not proud,' 1633), Hopkins's 'God's Grandeur' (1877), and Frost's 'Design' (1936) all hinge on volta. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate the turn and trace its reshaping.

Dramatic monologue is a lyric form in which a single speaker addresses an implied silent listener at a specific dramatic moment. Robert Browning perfected it — 'My Last Duchess' (1842) reveals the Duke of Ferrara through what he says to the marriage envoy and what he reveals without intending. Tennyson's 'Ulysses' (1842) and Eliot's 'Prufrock' (1915) anchor the modern tradition. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who identify dramatic monologue and explain how the silent auditor shapes meaning.

Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in nearby words. Old English verse (Beowulf, c. 8th–11th c.) was structured on alliterative meter; Hopkins revived alliterative density in 'Pied Beauty' (1877); Heaney's 'Digging' (1966) deploys alliteration to bind sound to physical action ('squelch and slap of soggy peat'). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who name alliteration precisely and explain how the device generates emphasis, mood, or thematic linkage rather than merely listing instances.

Catharsis is Aristotle's term in the Poetics (335 BCE) for the audience's purgation of pity and fear through witnessing tragedy. The exact mechanism — medical purge, emotional release, intellectual clarification — has been debated since the Renaissance. The deaths of Hamlet, Othello, and Lear (Shakespeare, 1601, 1604, 1606) aim at catharsis. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who deploy catharsis as interpretive frame. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) is earned through interrogation.

Hamartia is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the tragic protagonist's error or flaw that precipitates catastrophe — often translated 'tragic flaw' but better rendered 'missing the mark.' Oedipus's hubris (Sophocles, Oedipus Rex, c. 429 BCE), Macbeth's ambition (Shakespeare, 1606), and Othello's jealousy (Shakespeare, 1604) are classic hamartiai. AP Lit Q3 literary-argument FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward students who name hamartia precisely and interrogate whether it is moral failure or structural inevitability.

Soliloquy is a dramatic convention in which a character, alone on stage, speaks thoughts aloud to the audience. Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' (Hamlet 3.1, 1601), Macbeth's 'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow' (Macbeth 5.5, 1606), and Iago's confessional soliloquies in Othello (Shakespeare, 1604) are paradigmatic. The convention grants audience interior access. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who analyze soliloquy as crafted self-revelation generating character and theme.

Peripeteia is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the sudden reversal of fortune at the climax of tragedy. It works most powerfully when fused with anagnorisis — as in Oedipus Rex (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE), where Oedipus's discovery and his fall happen in the same moment. Macbeth's peripeteia comes when Birnam Wood moves to Dunsinane and he learns Macduff was 'from his mother's womb / Untimely ripped' (Shakespeare, 1606). AP Lit Q2 FRQs (6-point rubric, all rows) reward analysis of reversal.

Anagnorisis is Aristotle's term (Poetics, 335 BCE) for the protagonist's moment of recognition — the discovery of a previously hidden truth that transforms understanding. Oedipus's recognition of his patricide and incest in Oedipus Rex (Sophocles, c. 429 BCE) is the paradigmatic instance; Lear's recognition of Cordelia's love (Shakespeare, 1606) is another. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who locate anagnorisis and explain how recognition reshapes character, plot, and theme simultaneously.

Metaphor is a comparison without 'like' or 'as,' asserting that one thing IS another. I.A. Richards (The Philosophy of Rhetoric, 1936) named the parts: tenor and vehicle. Eliot's 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915) compares evening to 'a patient etherized upon a table' — a startling vehicle announcing modernist disorientation. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who unpack tenor and vehicle and explain how metaphor's compression generates thematic complexity. Sophistication (Row C, 1 pt) emerges through layered reading.

Simile is a comparison using 'like' or 'as.' Eliot's 'Let us go then, you and I, / When the evening is spread out against the sky / Like a patient etherized upon a table' ('The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock,' 1915), Plath's similes in 'Daddy' (1962), and Frost's 'My November Guest' all anchor on simile. AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish simile from metaphor and explain why explicit comparison — rather than metaphoric assertion — serves a particular interpretive purpose.

Motif is a recurring symbolic element — image, phrase, situation — that gathers thematic weight through repetition. Hamlet's references to disease and decay (Shakespeare, 1601), the rememory motif in Morrison's Beloved (1987), and the bee imagery in Plath's Ariel (1965) all build cumulative meaning. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who distinguish motif from symbol (motif emphasizes recurrence) and trace how repetition generates thematic argument across a work's length.

Symbol is a concrete object or image representing an abstract idea. Hawthorne's scarlet letter A (The Scarlet Letter, 1850), Conrad's Congo River (Heart of Darkness, 1899), Morrison's chokecherry tree on Sethe's back (Beloved, 1987), and Plath's bell jar (The Bell Jar, 1963) all carry symbolic weight beyond their literal presence. AP Lit Q2 prose-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify specific symbols and trace their interpretive resonance, earning sophistication (Row C) through layered reading.

Allusion is reference to another text, person, event, or work of art. Eliot's The Waste Land (1922) is built on dense allusion — Dante, Shakespeare, Augustine, Frazer's Golden Bough, Hindu Upanishads. Yeats alludes to Irish mythology and Byzantine theology; Plath alludes to Holocaust imagery in 'Daddy' (1962); Morrison alludes to the Middle Passage and Christian baptism in Beloved (1987). AP Lit Q1 poetry-analysis FRQs (6-point rubric, Row B, 4 pts) reward students who identify allusion and explain how reference deepens argument.

FRQ 1 is poetry analysis — students are given one short poem (occasionally two paired) and analyze how the poet uses literary elements to convey meaning. The 6-point rubric awards Row A (1 pt: thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary), Row C (1 pt: sophistication). Recent passages have included Frost's 'Acquainted with the Night' (1928), Heaney's 'Mid-Term Break' (1966), Bishop's 'The Fish' (1946), and Larkin's late lyrics. Strong essays anchor on 2–3 specific craft choices generating thematic argument.

FRQ 2 is prose analysis — students are given an excerpt of fiction or drama (typically 500–800 words) and analyze how the author uses literary elements to convey meaning. The 6-point rubric is identical to FRQ 1: Row A (1 pt: thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary), Row C (1 pt: sophistication). Recent passages have come from Faulkner's As I Lay Dying (1930), Atwood's Surfacing (1972), Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899), and Edith Wharton. Drama excerpts have included Williams's The Glass Menagerie (1944).

FRQ 3 is literary argument — students choose a full-length novel or play (from the suggested authors list or of comparable literary merit) and defend an interpretation of how the work develops a given theme. The 6-point rubric rewards Row A (1 pt: thesis), Row B (4 pts: evidence and commentary from memory), Row C (1 pt: sophistication). Strong students arrive with 3–5 thoroughly studied works — typically Shakespeare's Hamlet, a novel of social critique (Atwood, Morrison, Achebe), a bildungsroman (Hurston, Joyce), and a tragedy.

Forty minutes is allotted per FRQ within the 2-hour AP Lit Section II essay window (three FRQs total). Students should budget 8–10 minutes for annotation/planning, 25–30 minutes for drafting, and 2–5 minutes for revision. The 6-point rubric (Row B, 4 pts) rewards focused depth over breadth: strong essays anchor on 2–3 specific craft choices and trace them carefully rather than cataloguing every device. The sophistication point (Row C, 1 pt) requires complex interpretation transcending obvious readings.
